# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Comedies where the humour comes from the seriousness of characters and the film. Main example is the Naked Gun, the originals and the new one.

**Score:** 31
**Body:** Comedies where the humour comes from the seriousness of characters and the film. Main example is the Naked Gun, the originals and the new one.

Best way to describe is characters being super serious in absurd insane situations 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Airplane!
  - Surely you can't be serious.
    - I am serious… And don’t call me Shirley. 😊
2. Surely you have to include Airplane! (1980) as the finest example of deadpan humour
3. Best In Show 
4. Top Secret! (1984)
5. Airplane! (1980). The cast was chosen specifically because they were serious dramatic actors who played everything completely straight. That's the whole joke.
6. Johnny Dangerously,  Galaxy Quest,  Hot Shots,  Hot Shots Part Deux.
7. The series Police Squad..which the Naked Gun movies are based. 
Airplane
Blazing Saddles
Space Balls
History of the World part 1
8. burn after reading, fargo, o brother where art thou, the room, snatch, rock n rolla 

## 2. Nice light-hearted movie to watch with my girlfriend. Studio Ghibli, little miss Sunshine, moonrise kingdom, etc. she's really into sci-fi so bonus points for that.

**Score:** 26
**Body:** hey friends. I typically watch a lot of movies with heavier themes and drama. I want to pick a movie to watch with my girlfriend that's a bit lighter and makes your brain feel good. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Safety Not Guaranteed .. light hearted and Sci-fi
💯✌
2. Stardust
3. Fifth element!  
4. Land of the lost


Hitchhiker's guide to the galaxy


Galaxy quest
5. WALL-E?
6. Fantastic Mr. Fox
7. Amelie


Are you there God? It's me, Margaret 


Chungking Express


A Room With a View


The Secret of Kells
8. Flipped

Hunt for the Wilderpeople

Matilda

Booksmart

50 First Dates

  - Hunt for the Wilderpeople is so good! 
9. Batteries Not Included

## 3. looking for a movie to relate to. im 22. i hate my fucking life. have no family or friends and i am tryna find a reason to keep going. any movies like this?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** my dad never loved me enough to be around and my mom decided that meth is more important than me. please suggest me a movie i can cry too and relate too. i need this therapeutic release lol.

sorry for morbid title. something like the basketball diaries maybe?

please dont report my post. im not going to harm myself. i just get really sad about my life sometimes so i come on reddit and vent to strangers lol. its my only way to cope honestly. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This could be a terrible suggestion because it's really fucking weird, but Swiss Army Man recently gave me an epiphany that years of therapy didn't manage to do, so I'd recommend it. 

I hope things start getting better for you soon. 
2. The princess bride & stardust are two movies that give me comfort when I’m feeling down 
3. The fisher king. Jeff Bridges and Robin Williams are tremendous in this film. 
4. The Florida Project 
5. I’m sorry you’re having a hard time. Sorry if these don’t quite match what you’re going for.

A Beautiful Boy

Mysterious Skin

Th Perks of Being a Wallflower maybe?

Thirteen

The Art of Getting By

It’s Kind of a Funny Story

6. The Place Beyond the Pines

The Life of Chuck

The Goldfinch
7. I don’t have suggestions I can think of that aren’t already in this thread, but I do know in a way what you are going through (my entire 20’s was wasted battling with depression) and it can and does get better and you mean more to other people than you think. 
  - i hope this is true. thank you. 
8. little miss sunshine 
9. As good as it gets?

## 4. Looking for movies where a group has to survive together during a catastrophe

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I love movies that involve groups banding together to survive a disaster. I love the social decay, helplessness of it all, and desperation lol. Some that I can think that I enjoyed are, This is the End (2013), Leave the World Behind (2023), The Mist (2005), and Cloverfield (2008) to name a few. Mainly looking for newer movies and around 2000s or newer.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You asked for newer movies but there was a popular genre of disaster movies in 1970s worth watching. 
2. The Day After Tomorrow. 
3. I know you requested more recent, but if you haven’t seen it yet, I’m still going to recommend The Poseidon Adventure.
4. Zombieland.


5. Miracle Mile (1988)
6. Love and Monsters
7. The Grey, Flight of the Phoenix, The Day After Tomorrow, Dante's Peak, 28 Days Later, Deep Blue Sea, Deep Water Horizon, Apollo 13, Jurassic Park, Tremors, ALIVE, Everest, The Impossible, The Edge, Frozen, 47 Meters Down, The Road, Greenland, The Poseidon Adventure, A Quiet Place, Train to Buscan, Homeward Bound, The Brave Little Toaster, Land Before Time, Dinosaur, Honey I Shrunk The Kids.
8. Scouts Guide to the Zombie Apocalypse (2015)

9. Society of the Snow (2023) .... not based on a natural disaster but plane crash
  - And unlike the original movie about this event, Alive, this one is endorsed by the survivors. Many of them were consulted during the production of the movie. 

## 5. Looking for movies like Tropic Thunder.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Some things I loved about Tropic Thunder:

1. The parody of Hollywood and the film industry

2. Absurd characters with over-the-top personalities

3. Hilarious and unexpected action scenes

4. A mix of dark humor and satire

Can anyone recommend other films that have a similar vibe? Anything that’s like Tropic Thunder in terms of humor, action, or satire would be awesome.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The nice guys
  - Every Shane Black movie.

Shout out to The Last Action Hero.
2. Galaxy Quest.
3. Bowfinger – Steve Martin and Eddie Murphy sadly À mostly forgotten film. But quite funny. 

Hollywood Shuffle – one of Robert Townsend’s best. 
4. Get Shorty (1995) is the closest thing i can think of. Danny DeVitos character is supposedly based on Dustin Hoffman.
5. The Player is the greatest Hollywood film ever made.
6. This is the End. Especially the Hollywood bit. Has a bunch of actors that play themselves and make fun of themselves in the beginning
7. The producers?
8. Hail, Caesar 


Silent Movie
9. UHF

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Is Ryan Gosling the next Tom Hanks?

**Score:** 967
**Body:** Considering his filmography and overall likability I can see it.  

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He’s not so much like Tom Hanks, more of a Brad Pitt type career, but he’s not the “next” anything. He has his own lane. He’s 45 years old and has been starring in movies for over 20 years.
  - 45 years old and he looked like *that* in the Barbie movie? 
    - ![gif](giphy|3o7TKpKqAnFkO0nAVq)


    - Moisturizing matters!
  - Ryan seems to have both of Tom and Brad’s strengths.  He has Tom’s ability to be vulnerable, determined, funny, and insanely likable. He also has Brad’s ability to be insanely hot, a doofus, an asshole, can do action, and still be likable.

Ryan also has hidden talents: singing, dances, plays piano, does many of his own stunts…
2. No, but he’s the next Ryan Gosling!
3. I don't know about Tom Hanks, but he's certainly a better Canadian actor than Ryan Reynolds, that's for sure. 
  - He seems nicer than the other Ryan too
    - I don’t know why, but he strikes me as “actually nice” whereas Ryan Reynolds strikes me as “PR nice”
4. kinda? basically always the lead, very likable, good with comedy, drama and action.. great in rom coms.. can win awards, can open big box office numbers.. they are both very well rounded leading likable men, so I can see those similarities for sure.

## 2. If you were granted a wish for any single piece of movie memorabilia, what would it be?

**Score:** 620
**Body:** Movie memorabilia has grown from a niche hobby into a massive business, with millions of dollars exchanged at major auction houses such as Sotheby's, Julien's Auctions, Heritage Auctions, and Christie's.

The prop master genie just gave you the green light to make one wish. Which iconic movie prop, costume, storyboard, etc. would you choose, and why?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/bb8x2vrbcksg1.jpeg?width=418&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=6a887a38d39d7e1f9b4aef2e33b29a47160bae95
2. I would like The Bride’s Hattori Hanzo sword 
3. ![gif](giphy|7X3T4QPuWGSMU)
4. The cane from Citizen Kane 
  - Wait a minute…
5. Eleanor from gone in 60 seconds 
6. ![gif](giphy|knWj8IasyCf3q)
7. ![gif](giphy|VcRAN8c8wwOC4)
8. The [Glaive](https://youtu.be/Jp6zmsmkqSk?si=tlDkgbiGuopE-Dsn) from [Krull](https://boxd.it/29sG) (1983)
9. The bridge from Bridge over a river Kwai

## 3. How would you rank Emma Stone as an actress because of her body of work by far?

**Score:** 445

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’d put Emma Stone pretty high tbh. She’s done a mix of comedy and serious roles really well, and nothing feels forced. Not every movie is a hit, but her acting is usually solid.
  - Same here, she has a lot of range and i think that will take her very far in this career
    - It was her last few projects over the past couple years that really solidified her place as 1 of the greats in my mind. Props to the directors of those projects as well. 
  - Yes, she does good work in everything I've seen and from all reports is a super nice person to boot. Let's reward people who aren't assholes. 
2. If shes interested in a long career, she will have a long career.  

Think she's genuinely one of the best actresses currently working.  
3. She's excellent. 
4. Best under 40 IMO
5. My favorite movies are not her best acting. My favorite Emma movie is Easy A.
6. Usually not a fan, but she is a good actress. Loved Bugonia a lot. 
  - Bugonia is one movie that will make you remember for a while. 

## 4. What are some of the best "fight back" moments in film?

**Score:** 409
**Body:** *Capre Fear*, Cady is lit on fire as he lights his cigar. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/ve8jr2ix5isg1.png?width=250&format=png&auto=webp&s=3665a1cfd9ff3c1d8d99607871f2cfa11dfea46f

Ripley.
2. Marcellus Wallace in Pulp Fiction. We didn't see how it turned out, but his dialog was enough.
3. District 9 when Wikus is in the power armor.
4. https://i.redd.it/99vlj27qxhsg1.gif
5. Gotta be Rocky in 4 when he’s getting pummeled by Drago who seemed invincible and he finally lands his shot that cut him. Yes it’s obviously not a realistic fight but still one of my all time favorite movies and fights
6. Misery   

[“Here, you want it!!? You want it?!! Eat it!! Eat it til you choke, you sick twisted fuck!”](https://youtu.be/4-8adc39DV0?si=Rqs2Rd8N1WRMsUmT)
7. I'm not left-handed either
8. When George McFly balled up his fist n decked Biff. Back to the Future.
9. Neo wakes up as "The One" at the end of Matrix. Goes from dead from a gun shot to you can't even touch me. Great moment
10. Ralph beating up Skut Farkus in A Christmas Story is one of my all time favorite movie moments. It makes me feel feelings. That and when everyone sings La Marseilles in Casablanca 

## 5. It turns out this is just a regular bad movie.

**Score:** 370
**Body:** I thought Anaconda 2025 would be some kind of tongue in cheek parody of the original Anaconda, which was pretty bad stupid to start with, but this new version is bad in an unfunny way.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was harmless. The cast is too good for this to be truly bad.

Then cast is so good this could have been much, much better. 

It ended up being a fine, harmless, wildly forgettable movie. 
  - First time I’ve been disappointed in a Steve Zahn performance! He could have taken the movie to the next level if they gave him more to work with
    - The piss scene was the funniest.
      - Never a promising sentence. 
    - This Saving Silverman reunion was surprisingly tame.
      - Dude that's also a shame bc Saving Silverman was a great movie to have seen growing up. My younger bro and u loved that movie... how'd you kill her? We ate her... alive! 
2.  I actually loved this movie. Not in a critically great way, but I laughed and I felt the heart behind it.
3. I thought it was great, it was exactly the right amount of crazy for me.
4. It was better than I expected. The main cast definitely delivered.
5.  Nah, it was fun.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Stand by Me Ending

**Score:** 56
**Body:** Just got done seeing the re-release of Stand by Me. I was about the same age as the kids in the movie the first time I watched it, and loved it for its relatability at that time.

Seeing it again at 29 felt like a completely different experience. The ending was a complete punch in the gut:

“I will never have friends again like the friends I had when I was twleve years old. Jesus, does anyone?”

I had forgotten about it completely, and it didn’t move me enough at 12 years old to be memorable. I wasn’t expecting to be hit on such an emotional level 😭😭

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stand by Me is a fantastic example of great symbolism, where it's so perfectly intertwined with the story that you don't even notice it. It's a typical coming of age story about the death of childhood, reflected in their actual search for a dead child's body. The story and metaphor are one and the same. They literally spell it out ("the kid was dead") when [they finally come upon the child and gaze into the face forever marking the end of their adolescence.](https://youtu.be/ecsPydUbYGM?si=q024RsiyCwRS7SpN&t=66)
2. Yeah I hadn't watched that movie for over thirty years. I was roughly the same age as the kids in the movie when it came out. Watching it now, especially now that I have two young boys, was an emotional experience. It is such a perfect distillation of what being a young boy is like and captures the heightened emotion of youth, with all the beauty and ugliness of it. Maybe not a perfect film technically, but one of the best.
3. One thing I put together on my last rewatch was that after he types that he immediately goes and plays with his 12 year old son and his friend. Was Rob Reiner subtlety saying that spending time with your children is the best way to recapture the wonder of your childhood?
4. I’m planning on seeing this movie tonight. Is there any way you could let me know which theater chain you saw it at? And were there ads before it started? Sometimes rereleases don’t have them. My original question got deleted for not being long enough.

If you could just copy and paste something and respond so it doesn’t get deleted
  - saw it at regal! showtime listed as 7:15 and I want to say it started around 7:35. should be a standard trailer package so expect about 15 - 20 mins of previews :-)
5. I saw this again recently with a woman who had that laser-eye attention to detail. She said "who's that guy \[Dreyfuss\] meant to be?" "the kid, Gordie". "Really? Why are his eyes a different colour now? He looks nothing like him". "Goddamnit, this is one of my favourite films..." Actually, thinking about it, it would be amazing if they reshot those scenes with the grown up Wil Wheaton now.
6. .>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>

https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1s79t79/casual_discussion_thread_march_29_2026/

https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1s6xcu4/what_have_you_been_watching_week_of_march_29_2026/

Posts:

Threads must promote in-depth discussion.

Threads must point discussion in a specific direction.

## 2. I Made A List Featuring 250 Female Directors

**Score:** 45
**Body:** I recently realized i wasn't watching enough films directed by women so i decided to focus more on female directors. But i looked at some lists for recommendations and i realized everybody keep reccomending same directors over and over. I like films i have seen from household names like Gerwig, Coppola, Ramsay, Campion, Varda, Akerman etc. but i realized most lists features just these directors and i started to research for more. Finally i had made a list of 250 female directors that i have found interasting, in order to create an alternative canon of film history. Here is that list.

  


[https://boxd.it/QcHGM](https://boxd.it/QcHGM)

  


I am a person that mostly watches older films (i watch more pre-1970 movies than post-2000 movies) and i didn't see many directors from older eras in those reccomendations, Varda is pretty much the only person that has been canonized from the first 70 years of film history. One of my main questions was did no female directors exist during that era or were they just forgotten, waiting to be rediscovered. Most lists were focused on the female directors that had made their debut in 21st century, i was curious about the forgotten older directors. At the end, i have found 37 directors that i represented with a film before the 1970. There are also some people who are being represented by their later works that made movies before 1970.

  


I included only one film for every director as a representitive of their careers. I only included features which means no Deren etc. I did not include films women co-directed with men. I realize some of those are very important but if i had opened that door films that are famous with a male directors name like Turin Horse, City Of God and Slumdog Millionaire would be eligible for the list. That could be a seperate list. 

  


This list is both a list for highlighting interasting female filmmakers and a long term personal watchlist for me. Therefore, i did choose less popular movies by some directors because i had already seen other more popular ones. This means no Beau Travail for Denis, no Anatomy Of A Fall for Triet, no Piano for Campion etc. This list has only 6 movies that i have seen before. I tried to avoid them but three of them were the only features by their directors, two of them were the most "popular" film by a lesser known director and i wasn't sure that i could find their other movies and the last one was someone that only has two solo directorial efforts and i have watched them both. I added those because i thought they deserve more attention, i haven't included popular films i have already seen so no Aftersun no Gerwig etc. 

  


I especially tried to avoid the list being too anglo-centric, i believe i have succeeded with that effort. We live in a world that still wrestles with the remnants of colonialism, i wanted my list to portray a diverse selection of women. Popularity of people like Varda, Campion, Coppola and Gerwig is tied to them living in the west. This does not mean that their works are any less impactful, it just means that we don't see the works of other directors that are just as good because they aren't from the western world. My list has directors from 75 different countries with 44 different languages. 72 of the 250 movies are in English.

  


Female directors are less likely to have extensive auteur filmographies, there are lots of directors on the list that couldn't make a  new movie for years(on some cases never) after their well recieved first feature. Filmmakers from the eastern bloc are an interasting exception in that regard, them being employed by government gave them a more steady working opportunity. Female directors of western countries usually didn't get another chance after one failure. If they are independent filmmakers that finance their own movies one failure is their disaster while if they are working for a studio studio stops hiring them because they made the studio lose money. Larisa Shepitko, who died while she was 41, has made four films; same amount with 93 year old Elaine May. 

  


What are your thoughts about this list or my comments on it? Did i miss any important female directors? What are your thoughts on the history of female directors in general?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can’t believe I didn’t know Joan Chen directed movies. Ive only seen about a dozen of these you have listed. Fantastic job from my limited perspective. 

It’s maddening how many great women directors got sidelined or outright in “directors jail”. I know other art mediums also had and have rampant sexism, but film in particular is so galling. I remember when the first Twilight came out and was a rousing success, Catherine Hardwicke got replaced for the sequel, that was such bullshit.
  - Lots of movies on my list is directed by well know actresses, on most countries transitioning to directing after acting in couple of well regarded features is easier than starting as a director. On my list there also films by well known actresses such as Kinuyo Tanaka, Anna Karina, Jeanne Moreau, Delphine Seyrig, Barbara Streisand, Liv Ullmann etc. Also there are lots of directors who are famous actresses in their national cinema but we don't know them. 
    - Great list. Another recent movie star turned film director is the Taiwanese actress Shu Qi, who made her fame in a lot of Greater China  movies won the Best Director Award in Busan Festival last year for her directorial debut “Girl”. I am really happy for her. 
Quite a journey for somebody who rose to fame by being coerced into posing for nude pictures and then became a movie star, and a respected actress who starred in Hoi Hsiao Hsien’s movie and at the end successful director. Way to go! 
2. I'd like to add: as a fellow Turk, happy to see Turkish directors represented as well!


Two of my favorite films in the last decade are Booksmart by Olivia Wilde (another actor turned director) and Love, Lies, Bleeding (co-written and) directed by Rose Glass.
  - I tried not to overrepresent Turkish directors, naturally i know more movies in a language i know compared to the languages i don't know. But i believe 7 directors i included from Turkey are as important as the ones from the other countries. 

## 3. The message of Falling Down is simply don't be like William Foster, be like Martin Prendergast.

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Foster doesn't realize that he was the problem until he watches the family videotapes, and later when he's confronted by Prendergast at the pier. Instead of facing the consequences of his own actions, he committed suicide by cop. Prendergast truly didn't want to kill him, he just wanted to help him, he was trying to reason with Foster by saying "Think about your daughter. Don't you want to see her grow up?" And Foster was like "No. i'm not going to jail. I want you to kill me so my daughter can get the insurance." He has some compassion for the man that shot his partner earlier.  

The message of the film is, it's okay to be annoyed at things that bother you in life, but as long as you are aware of your "monster" and know your capacity for harm but actively choose to fight it, you are better/stronger than most people. You could blame anyone else (society, education, observation) but if you choose to instead do yourself better, that's real courage and authorship. In other words, Foster could've done better if he simply adjusted his own expectations and didn't bark up the wrong tree. Prendergast could've went down the dark path that Foster went, but decided not to. And that's why the film ends with him deciding to remain a cop. 

The film's screenwriter Ebbe Roe Smith said: "Falling Down, I think, if anything it's about, maybe, the point of views, and the problem that strife in society happens when you're unable to appreciate the point of view of another person. You're unable to put yourself in their shoes. You lock yourself off away from that. And people start wars over it. I think it’s a lot of that. Compromising is the secret to a society, and when people start to shut themselves off from it, then there's only one place that energy can go, and it's into a negative space, into a negative area. And it hardens opinion, and nothing is done. Nothing is pushed forward. The walls are built up, and there's no place for that energy to go but to a bad place." 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't think any single character in the movie is a model. Pendergast has great qualities, and in particular he is a guy who is able to suffer his wife. But that's also the motivation of Michael Douglas in the movie. He wants to take care of his wife and kids. He goes around town trying to paternalize people about how they should behave, which is why Pendergast is mocked too. It's a parallel, but I don't think one is particularly a model over the other. Pendergast has a wife that demands him, so his family holds on, and he has a badge that allows him to paternalize people who do wrong. Otherwise, without power, without someone demanding him to do that, he might have been like D-fens.
  - I think you're misreading Pendergast a bit. 

D-Fens is a man who feels powerless and decides to start taking out his frustrations on everyone else. Pendergast is the inverse, he has the power to change things, but he allows everyone else to take their frustrations out on him. His wife controls his life, the other officers play pranks on him, his captain bullies him, and his response is to give up and run away from it all. The climax of the movie isn't just D-Fens realizing "I'm the bad guy?" it's also Pendergast realizing he's the good guy. (And I'm not making some "good guy with a gun" argument here, even though the movie can clearly be read that way. I'm talking about the broader responsibilities Pendergast is entrusted with as a police officer)

At the start of the movie, both characters believe the problems of the world are not their responsibility, and by the end, they both realize they were wrong.
  - Well, no, obviously they're not models. But, it's a life message. Because if anybody becomes like William Foster, then they'll not only destroy everyone around them, but also destroy themselves too. 

You're right, it is a paralell. Ebbe Roe Smith intelligently wrote Foster and Prendergast to be mirrors of each other. 

Foster has an unhealthy obsession with a 1950s idealic American way of life that doesn't exist. And I feel that's what alienated him from his own family. His unrealistic expectations. 
    - Another movie from the 1990s that deals with a mythical 1950s and it's characters flaws for that is the comedy Blast From The Past starring Brendan Fraser. His parents build a bomb shelter in the 60s and he lives his entire life in that shelter until 1999 when he emerges as an adult 35 year old man. Its a love story that shows how that 50s idealism could work- if your in isolation! 
Pleasantville also works within this dynamic but switched. Kids from the 90s go into the 1950s black and white television universe which at first seemed ideal (to the brother at least who was obsessed) only to realize that worlds constraints.

And that's what maybe was in the psyche of William Foster as you argue here. I agree. He was contrasting the ideal (flawed) with reality (also very flawed). He was like Huxley's Savage (A Brave New World) aware of everyone's flawed thinking and programming (or lack) yet he never considered looking inward until the videotapes showed him. Unable to reconcile with his flaws. 

That's the dilemma of the strong silent type. "Do you hear them? All those dead silent types?" They left no story....

That's Falling Down.
      - how did you come up with this idea
        - Huxley's forward to either Islands or a newer edition to BNW talks about the Savage not having a way out and how that bothered him enough to write Islands about expats trying to build a new society with ideals collectively, only to fail running into a more primitive version of modernity that they were forced to reconcile with. 

Its conciet; but that's where I would begin.

The 90s were an end to a Century. That was huge. Alot of idealism from the middle of that century still affected the people and ideals leading into the next century. I bring up those films as a contrast to the idea of individuals reconciling with a society that has moved on from their beliefs.

Best version of Islands is DeCaprios "The Beach".
    - His expectations aren't really about him or America imo. I understand what Ebbe Roe Smith said, but it doesn't mean that Foster should wear the stigma of the fault.

For exemple, Foster deals with gang members. That is nothing to celebrate about the "new society". And he acts very calmy in that situation, without any particular judgment, he's understanding of the dynamic of gangs to the point that he's okay to leave without issue and talk them out of stupid behavior, which they choose anyway. Then he spares them after being the target of a drive by. It's not the behavoir of a guy who clings onto a gone era. Or when he goes to the burger joint and scores point with the girl employee by forcing the manager to stop the hypocrite corporate smile. He understands very much the issue that everyone agrees on, he's not living his own fantasy. In this case, the manager is the one that doesn't take responsibility for his behavior, hiding behind bullshit policies without considering the consequences on people around him. The employees suffer, the people who come in hoping for a good moment are disappointed, and the whole thing is spoiled for everybody. But it only becomes aggravating when you face someone hiding behind that corporate power to slight people without any human consideration. Then it becomes a personnal issue, because they're not treated humanly.

Pendergast takes that exact kind of shit in his job, by the way. His colleagues and even his boss are attrocious people. Isn't there in theme here? Something to criticize maybe even more than Foster? Foster is worthy enough to be the main character, that's why I think being judgmental of his downfall is too easy. He's been a Pendergast for all of his life too, a lot of people admire someone who finally decides to step up against the constant abuses of the morally dubious. Somehow Foster gets criticized for it, even though it's clearly fiction. I don't know why, I think between him and Batman, the only difference is that Foster is clumsy and pathetic. So he doesn't receive any empathy. That's the whole story, the whole theme. The cycle of violence is being perpetrated by people who look up to the wrong thing.
2. Obviously the message is that he should have built some kind of Iron Man suit or maybe a better engineered "Killdozer" like Marvin John Heemeyer, duh. Like have a plan, dude, THAT'S the message.   


## 4. How do you read the ending of Marty Supreme?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Marty Supreme has somewhat of an ambiguous ending in that the director leaves us thinking about what comes next for Marty. Here we had a guy obsessed with table tennis and making it his sole purpose in life. In this pursuit he leaves behind family, friends, jobs, lovers and even a potential child. All these things are like roadblocks in the pursuit of glory in table tennis.

But the events of the film seem to wear Marty down by the end. He does not get the chance to taste glory in table tennis the way he envisioned. It's a very humbling turn of events where he goes through very degrading moments in such a pursuit. He does get the win over the world champion after all but the way it is framed seems to me that Marty feels the victory to be hollow. That after all the ordeals he went through to get to Endo (the best player at that moment), the win over him, notwithstanding the circumstances, should have been the greatest feeling of his life. For that single moment he was the best player in the world by beating the World Champion himself. But it does not seem to be the case. It's a satisfying feeling but incomplete.

The way I read the ending is that Marty sees his child and only then feels true emotion and joy. It's not the victory / table tennis that gives him that. Even though he was chasing glory of table tennis all the time and running away from his relationships. But it's the very thing he refused to accept, his girl and his child, that makes him feel.

That only when he was able to see what's on the other side of being the best player in the world and fulfilling his ego is when he realized that there is something even greater than that. 

Maybe this is an optimistic take but I choose to see it this way.

One small change I would have made to the climactic match is that while Marty his playing, he gets a couple of flashbacks of a shot Rachel as she is taken away for surgery in the hospital. Maybe it's cheesy but just to convey (if my above take is correct) that Marty did have love and sympathy for Rachel and their child after all.

Thoughts on the ending?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. imo there’s no way the person we’ve been watching hustle lie and manipulate his way into a second-tier mostly invisible win that marks the abrupt end of it all is now, suddenly, magically, transformed into a completely different person who now suddenly, magically, sees fathering his newborn as his new purpose in life

my read is that the baby is the symbol of the end of his table tennis dream, and Marty is feeling a confluence of emotions all at once when he sees it. in that moment he is coming to the realization that everything he thought he was living for is over. there is exhaustion, elation at winning, sadness and fear at knowing it’s the end, relief that at least somebody stuck around and still wants to be with him, and maybe a small glimmer of hope for himself in those tears

but an all-encompassing glee for his newborn baby and finally a chance to become father of the year? no, I don’t see that in the tears from the character we just spent 2.5 hours with
  - I don’t think he’s transformed or a changed man (I actually kind of think the opposite) but I do think he feels invigorated or focused. And I do think in that moment he wants nothing more than to be a good dad. I don’t necessarily think he will be and I don’t think the movie is saying he will be. But I think the desire/internal drive is communicated.

I think flawed people can still aspire to be better. (Even if only momentarily).
    - yeah I agree there is a kind of hope in it. Like, somebody with Marty’s drive can push through these moments, and you hope he can get realigned to value, pay attention to, and care about the people who are still there with him. especially his relationship with Rachel, they’re both cut from much of the same cloth, and I got such a young-and-dumb firey vibe from both of them. you hope they’ll be able to look back, shake their heads, and roll their eyes about this time
    - What I don't understand about the read of "Marty can't change" is why they think we are supposed to assume this is a complete and total instant turnaround for him rather than the beginning of a shift in priorities.  The ending in no way indicates that he's going to stop being a hustling rude little pissant, but it does seem to indicate that he might continue to be who he is in a slightly less self absorbed way going forward.  I imagine he'll still be coming up with schemes left and right but now in pursuit of something less grandiose than "greatness."  More focused on his real responsibilities than previously, but still tackling them in a frustratingly Marty way.
      - I agree wholeheartedly. There’s a multitude of reactions/takes saying his “transformation” or whatever isn’t believable. It’s like we didn’t see anything after that moment! We don’t know. All we see is that moment at the end. 

I guess perhaps they don’t think someone who acts as self interested and petulant as he does for 2+ hrs can even have that kind of revelation but I don’t believe that. Honestly in a larger sense maybe it’s the fact I refuse to believe that people can’t change (or in this case want to change), that the end resonates with me.
  - This is my read too. It’s a mix of emotions, a lot of exhaustion. It’s bittersweet and I’m skeptical that he’ll be a changed man just because he had a kid
2. I don’t know if it’s joy so much as relief.  The whole movie he’s chased by the self inflicted notion of his own greatness, of what he COULD be.  The ending is when he realizes whatever he was going to be has already happened, he’s not going to be an official champion or international superstar who redefines the game, he’s already peaked.  The unofficial rematch in Japan was it.

He’s relieved that the chaos is over, because what was causing obsessive behavior was driven by deep insecurity.  Because he now knows his peak is in the past that insecurity is gone, he’s knows exactly how good he is/was.  It’s like he’s getting to read his obituary 40 years early and he’s fine with it.
3. Fantastic ending. Marty sobbing at seeing his kid in a room of crying babies while “Everybody Wants To Rule the World” plays. Then we’re just left with the sound of all the babies crying over each other. 

Stuck with me for a while. The entire film explores the consequences of rampant American exceptionalism and ambition, only to close with a brand new generation of Americans all crying over each other, set to enter the same suffocatingly competitive arena that drove Marty to the brink of insanity.

4. I think he realises that he has to grow up. 

All the trouble he gets into is fine when he’s causing harm to others that aren’t really his responsibility. 

Now he has a child. The decisions he makes actually have consequences. 
5. My take was that Marty felt complete by the end. He starts off wanting glory and accolades and depraves himself in search of it. I think by the end he realizes all he really needs is the confirmation of his greatness and love.

## 5. I didn’t like Blue Velvet after watching it

**Score:** 4
**Body:** I watched it for the first time a couple days ago. I didn’t find it that deep, or disturbing… Not to say I don’t like it, but I just didn’t get the hype. If you asked me immediately after watching I’d say it’s a fine film, nothing special. I probably won’t talk about it again.

THAT SAID

That was 3 days ago and I cannot for the life of me get this movie out of my head. It’s to the point that I feel the need to let this off my chest and share it. I don’t want to get into a long pretentious review of the film or why this and why that, but it just stuck with me in a way not a lot of movies have. So many of the scenes, the acting, the score… are just haunting. Such a beautiful film that changed my mind over my feelings for it by just being in the background of my mind. I haven’t rewatched it, or read about it. It’s just there. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You didn’t miss it. Some films don’t hit in the moment—they settle in and start working on you after. The fact it stayed with you like that means it did exactly what it was built to do. A lot of people chase movies that impress them immediately. The ones that linger like this are the ones that actually change how you see things.
  - FUKC THAT SHITE PABST BLUE RIBBON 
    - Lol now I'm picturing Frank Booth with a Scottish brogue 
  - This. The growers tend to have more staying power of my all time favorites list. Brb going to watch Blue Velvet again.
    - I actually thought Blue Velvet was kinda goofy the first time—especially the Roy Orbison scene and Dennis Hopper in that weird 70s spy gear—but it was like my brain was refusing to process how dark it was. Then it seeped in later during reflection and hit way harder, especially everything at Dorothy’s apartment and the overall undertones. Can't help but think that's exactly how Lynch designed it.
  - Even Taxi Driver works the exact same way. It just stays with you and that is all you're thinking about even days after watching it the first time.
    - Eyes Wide Shut was a big one like this for me too. I thought it didn't click with me, but then the next few days I could not stop thinking about it. It called me back and then it blew my mind and became one of my most beloved films. 
2. You must drink Heineken.

I crack up every time he calls Dean Stockwell’s weirdo character, “suave”. Really beautiful story, with the mother and daughter. It has a fairy tale quality. Timeless.
  - Fuck that. Pabst Blue Ribbon!
3. I was kinda the same with BV the first time. Kinda wasn't sure how I felt about it or if I even liked it much. Now consider it a profoundly haunting masterpiece. Definitely a trip into the abyss.
